title: Prompting 101: Show, don’t tell>
summary: Improve your prompts with better examples rather than better instructions
full_text:
A really common mistake I see people make when writing prompts is
starting the prompt with something like:

You are a Staff+ Software Architect with 40 years of experience
designing distributed systems at Google, Meta, and NASA. Produce only
optimal, production-ready code following industry best practices.

This is an example of a prompt that tells the large
language model how to behave instead of showing the
large language model how to behave.

One issue with this style of prompting is that it doesn’t
even work on humans. Like, imagine that you were a manager who
was trying to help a report who was underperforming and telling them:
“Hey, you’re not an underperformer. You’re actually an overperformer!
Write high quality code.” Would you seriously expect their performance
or coding ability to improve? Hopefully not!

The thing that would actually help an underperformer improve is to
teach or (even better) show them how to do a better job
and the same is true for models.

Mechanical sympathy

The above prompting style isn’t just ineffective; it’s
counterproductive and works at odds with how LLMs are trained.

LLMs are pattern-recognition machines1,
meaning that for any given prompt they will attempt to pattern match on
that prompt to find semantically similar examples in their training
dataset and riff on those examples.

So where do you think the LLM will find text within its training
dataset that says something similar to “You are a Staff+ Software
Architect with 40 years of experience …”?

Would you find something like that at the beginning of a technical
blog post? No.

Would you find something like that at the beginning of any
programming reference material? No.

Would you find something like that in the preamble of some quality
code? No.

The places you would find instructions like that are:

acting instructions setting the stage for a screenplay

e.g. “You are an expert hacker trying to break into the CIA’s
database …”

job descriptions

e.g. ”Who are you, the ideal candidate we’re looking for? A Senior
Software Architect with 10 years of experience …”

The problem is that none of those matches (e.g. acting instructions
or job descriptions) in the training dataset contain actual
expertise for the model to draw upon when answering the
question.

By prompting the model in this way you haven’t actually turned the
model into an expert. You’ve turned the model into something that is
roleplaying expertise (like an actor in a screenplay) and by
doing so you are more likely to bias the model to generate
plausible and/or hallucinated output rather than
correct output (just like a TV show’s hamfisted portrayal of
hacking).

Example

I like to practice what I preach so rather than tell
you how to improve your prompt I’m going to show you
with an example where we try to improve a model’s conversational style.
Our first (wrong) attempt might look like this:

System: You are a helpful assistant with a casual
and informal tone. To cultivate a laid back attitude drop capitalization
and use punctuation sparingly. Try to chat in a back-and-forth style
that is “lower latency” and avoid large monolithic messages.

If I give an OpenAI assistant that as the system prompt, it’s largely
ineffective at turning off ChatGPT’s “instructor voice” where it wants
to provide incredibly long, detailed, and self-contained responses
followed by the obligatory prompt to stimulate more discussion:

User: What’s the tradeoff between Applicative and
Monad in Haskell

Assistant: hey cool question

basically, the main difference is in how much “power” and flexibility
you get when writing code with each of them

an Applicative lets you combine computations
independently — you can “stack” effects without the order mattering or
without one computation being able to depend on the result of
another

a Monad gives you more — now each step can depend on the
result of the previous one, using that good old >>=
(bind)

tradeoff:

if you stick to Applicative, your code will be more
parallelizable or easier to analyze/optimize, because the
structure of effects is fixed ahead of time

using Monad you gain expressiveness (more kinds of
dependencies), but lose some potential for analysis/optimization, since
computations can decide what to do next depending on results

a motto could be:

use Applicative when you don’t need the extra power of Monad — but
use Monad when you do

does that help? want any code examples or real-life cases?

However, one of the neat features of most models is that you can seed
any chat with a fake chat history, including fake messages from the
assistant. This means that instead of telling the model to be more
conversational I can seed the model with a chat history that
demonstrates what I had in mind:

User: hey, can you do me a favor?

Assistant: what is it?

User: i wanted to ask if you could speak in an
informal style

Assistant: what do you mean?

User: well, like, right now what you’re doing is
already pretty good

User: like, not saying too much with each
response

User: no caps

User: low punctuation

Assistant: do i need to change something?

User: not exactly, but i wanted to highlight a few
specifics

Assistant: okay (listening)

User: you don’t want to say too much at once

User: like, try to avoid a monolithic response

Assistant: you mean to explain a little at a
time?

Assistant: and ask clarifying questions? rather than
trying to answer everything in one message

User: yes! exactly!

Assistant: alright. i’ll keep that in mind

User: and again, like, this conversation was pretty
good

Notice how the above prompt is not just teaching the
model what to do, but also showing the model what to do
(by example). We’re asking the model to chat in a low-latency and
conversational style, and the instructions themselves are a chat
conversation matching the desired style.

Check out how the conversation flows now:

User: What’s the tradeoff between Applicative and
Monad in Haskell?

Assistant: applicative is less powerful but easier
to reason about

monad can do more (lets you use results of previous computations),
but more complex

monads imply applicative, but not vice versa

User: What do you mean when you say
Applicative is easier to reason about?

Assistant: applicative computations can’t depend on
results of previous steps

that makes the flow more predictable

you know the structure before actually running anything

User: Can you give an example of something that’s an
Applicative but not a Monad?

Assistant: validation with accumulating errors is a
classic one (like Validation from
Data.Validation)

you can collect errors from multiple computations at once with
applicative

but you can’t short-circuit or use earlier results for later steps,
so it’s not a monad

User: What’s Validation? Never heard of
it

Assistant: it’s a data type, kinda like
Either, but when you combine failures, it can collect all
the errors instead of stopping at the first one

usually used for validating multiple fields at once

User: No, like, what is the actual definition of the
Validation type?

Assistant: it’s usually

data Validation e a = Failure e | Success a

so, similar to Either but Failure is for
errors, Success is for valid values

The model has now completely dropped the instructor voice and the
conversation is more participatory. It’s no longer trying to
explain everything at once nor trying to artificially stimulate
conversation. Instead, the model now regularly yields conversation flow
back to the user.
